content.text: Applying security patches to an agentic AI platform requires a process that goes beyond the standard 'bump the version, run unit tests, deploy' workflow. An LLM SDK patch may change the API response format in a way that silently breaks the agent's tool-call parsing. A vector database patch may alter the index format in a way that degrades retrieval recall. A serialisation library patch may change checkpoint format in a way that corrupts in-progress agent sessions. These failure modes are not caught by standard regression suites and require AI-specific post-patch validation.

A security patch cadence for agentic AI dependencies is the documented policy that defines: which dependency categories are subject to what severity-based patching SLAs, what testing must pass before a patch can be deployed, and what additional AI-specific validation is required for LLM SDK, vector database, prompt-processing library, and serialisation library patches.

The cadence operates at two levels. The standard level follows the CVSS score: CRITICAL (9.0+) patches must be applied within 48 hours, HIGH (7.0-8.9) within 7 days, MEDIUM (4.0-6.9) within 30 days, LOW within 90 days. The genAI-specific level adds overrides: prompt injection vulnerabilities are treated as CRITICAL regardless of CVSS score, LLM SDK patches always require a full eval suite run, and vector database patches require a retrieval quality validation before deployment.

The cadence is not just a policy document — it is implemented as automated scanning, policy enforcement in CI, and deployment processes. A dependency that has an open CRITICAL CVE blocks all deployments until it is patched. The policy is enforced technically, not socially.

Agentic AI systems have a larger and more complex dependency surface than standard web applications. A typical agentic AI platform depends on: LLM client libraries (anthropic, openai, litellm), vector databases (chromadb, qdrant, pinecone), orchestration frameworks (langchain, llamaindex), prompt templating engines (jinja2, mako), tool integration libraries, and standard web framework and utility libraries. Each of these is a potential vulnerability entry point, and several of them are pathways to AI-specific attacks that standard security tools do not classify accurately.

Prompt injection vulnerabilities are a prime example. A vulnerability in a prompt templating library that allows user input to escape its expected context may have a CVSS score of 5.0 (MEDIUM) because the CVSS scoring system does not account for the fact that in an agentic system, escaping prompt context means controlling the agent's next action. The standard cadence would schedule this patch for the monthly batch. The genAI-specific cadence overrides it to CRITICAL and requires a 48-hour patch.

Without a documented cadence, security patches accumulate in the dependency audit report and are addressed ad-hoc when someone notices them, which typically means they are addressed when an auditor raises them or when an incident occurs. A systematic cadence with automated SLA tracking converts this ad-hoc process into a predictable, auditable maintenance practice.

Severity-based SLAs ensure that CRITICAL vulnerabilities are addressed within 48 hours while MEDIUM vulnerabilities are batched into the monthly release, avoiding both under-response and over-response.
GenAI-specific overrides ensure that prompt injection vulnerabilities and LLM API behaviour changes are treated with appropriate urgency regardless of their CVSS score.
Requiring a full eval suite run after LLM SDK patches catches silent API behaviour regressions that standard unit tests do not detect.
Automated CVE scanning with timestamped reports and patch deployment records produce the evidence that compliance audits require.
MEDIUM and LOW patches batched into monthly releases reduce deployment frequency for low-risk changes while maintaining systematic coverage.

Requiring a full eval suite run after every LLM SDK patch adds cost and latency to the patch process. For daily patch scanning runs, this may be prohibitive — schedule eval runs only when the scan finds a vulnerability in an LLM-related package.
CRITICAL patches with a 48-hour SLA may not allow adequate regression testing time. The emergency patch process must explicitly document the acceptable level of reduced testing for critical patches.

Tight version pinning (which enables predictable patching) conflicts with using framework features that require later versions. The patch cadence assumes that versions are pinned and bumped deliberately.
Validating retrieval recall before and after a vector DB patch requires a reference dataset and a quality metric. Building this test infrastructure is an upfront investment.
The patch cadence pipeline has three phases: detection, patch and test, and deploy.

Detection runs daily via a CI job that executes pip-audit against all requirements files and npm audit for Node dependencies. The output is classified by CVSS score and against the genAI-specific override rules. Vulnerabilities at CRITICAL or HIGH severity trigger a PagerDuty alert to the on-call engineer and create a Jira ticket automatically. MEDIUM and LOW vulnerabilities are batched into the weekly dependency review meeting.

Patch and test begins immediately for CRITICAL vulnerabilities and within the SLA window for HIGH. The patch script upgrades the affected package, runs the standard regression suite, and — for LLM-related packages — runs the post-patch genAI compatibility tests and the full eval suite. If any test fails, the patch branch is blocked and the failure is escalated.

Deployment follows the standard change management process for HIGH and MEDIUM vulnerabilities: patch branch reviewed and merged, CI gates pass, deployment to staging, soak period, deployment to production. For CRITICAL vulnerabilities, an emergency change process is followed: abbreviated review (two senior engineers), staging bypass allowed if testing passed in CI, immediate production deployment.

attrs.alt: Security patch cadence: severity lanes (CRITICAL 48h, HIGH 7d, MEDIUM 30d) with AI-specific considerations for LLM SDK, vector DB, and prompt library patches

content.text: The diagram shows three severity lanes with distinct timelines and deployment processes. The AI-specific patch considerations at the bottom highlight the additional validation required for agentic AI dependency categories beyond standard regression testing.

This cadence applies to any agentic AI system in production. The specific SLA windows and genAI-specific overrides should be calibrated to the risk profile of the system: a customer-facing agent with access to sensitive data warrants tighter SLAs and more comprehensive post-patch testing than an internal-only tool.

For development and staging environments, the cadence may be relaxed: CRITICAL patches within 1 week, others batched into weekly releases. The production cadence should be the strictest, with staging serving as the validation environment for the patch process itself.
Step 1: Define and codify the policy

# config/patch-policy.yaml
# Security patch SLA policy for agentic AI platform dependencies

severity_slas:
  critical:
    cvss_threshold: 9.0
    patch_sla_hours: 48
    deployment_process: emergency
    eval_suite_required: true
    release_block: true   # block all other releases until patched

  high:
    cvss_threshold: 7.0
    patch_sla_days: 7
    deployment_process: standard
    eval_suite_required: true
    release_block: false

  medium:
    cvss_threshold: 4.0
    patch_sla_days: 30
    deployment_process: batch_monthly
    eval_suite_required: false
    release_block: false

  low:
    cvss_threshold: 0.0
    patch_sla_days: 90
    deployment_process: next_release
    eval_suite_required: false
    release_block: false

genai_overrides:
  # Treat prompt injection CVEs as CRITICAL regardless of CVSS
  - vuln_pattern: "prompt.injection|SSRF.*agent|indirect.injection"
    override_severity: critical

  # LLM SDK updates always require eval suite
  - packages: ["anthropic", "openai", "langchain", "litellm"]
    eval_suite_required: true

  # Vector DB patches require retrieval quality test
  - packages: ["chromadb", "qdrant-client", "pinecone-client", "weaviate-client"]
    additional_tests: ["tests/quality/test_retrieval_recall.py"]

# scripts/security/dependency-scan.sh
#!/bin/bash
# Scan dependencies for CVEs and classify by severity.
# Exits non-zero if CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs found.

set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Security Dependency Scan ==="
echo "Date: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"

# Python dependencies
echo "--- Python (pip-audit) ---"
pip-audit \
  --requirement requirements.txt \
  --requirement requirements-llm.txt \
  --format json \
  --output pip-audit-report.json

python3 - << 'PYEOF'
import json, sys

with open("pip-audit-report.json") as f:
    report = json.load(f)

critical = [v for dep in report.get("dependencies", [])
            for v in dep.get("vulns", [])
            if v.get("fix_versions") and
               any(a.get("cvss_v3", {}).get("base_score", 0) >= 9.0
                   for a in v.get("aliases", []))]

high = [v for dep in report.get("dependencies", [])
        for v in dep.get("vulns", [])
        if v.get("fix_versions") and
           any(7.0 <= a.get("cvss_v3", {}).get("base_score", 0) < 9.0
               for a in v.get("aliases", []))]

if critical:
    print(f"CRITICAL CVEs found ({len(critical)}): {[v['id'] for v in critical]}")
    sys.exit(2)
if high:
    print(f"HIGH CVEs found ({len(high)}): {[v['id'] for v in high]}")
    sys.exit(1)
print("No CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs found.")
PYEOF

# Node.js dependencies (for frontend or tooling)
if [ -f package-lock.json ]; then
    echo "--- Node.js (npm audit) ---"
    npm audit --audit-level=high --json > npm-audit-report.json || true
fi

echo "=== Scan complete ==="

# scripts/security/patch-workflow.py
"""
Automates the security patch workflow:
1. Identify CVEs from scan output
2. Create a patch branch
3. Apply patches (pip install --upgrade)
4. Run regression tests
5. Create a PR with the CVE details
"""
import json, subprocess, sys
from datetime import datetime

def load_vulnerabilities(report_path: str) -> list[dict]:
    with open(report_path) as f:
        report = json.load(f)
    vulns = []
    for dep in report.get("dependencies", []):
        for vuln in dep.get("vulns", []):
            if vuln.get("fix_versions"):
                vulns.append({
                    "package": dep["name"],
                    "installed": dep["version"],
                    "fix_version": vuln["fix_versions"][0],
                    "vuln_id": vuln["id"],
                    "description": vuln.get("description", ""),
                })
    return vulns

def apply_patches(vulns: list[dict]) -> list[str]:
    upgraded = []
    for vuln in vulns:
        pkg = f"{vuln['package']}=={vuln['fix_version']}"
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["pip", "install", "--upgrade", pkg],
            capture_output=True, text=True
        )
        if result.returncode == 0:
            upgraded.append(pkg)
            print(f"Patched: {pkg}")
        else:
            print(f"FAILED to patch {pkg}: {result.stderr}")
    return upgraded

def run_regression_suite() -> bool:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "-m", "pytest", "tests/", "-m", "not slow",
         "--tb=short", "-q"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(result.stdout)
    return result.returncode == 0

def run_eval_suite() -> bool:
    """Re-run agent eval suite after LLM SDK patches."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "-m", "pytest", "evals/", "-q",
         "--json-report", "--json-report-file=eval-results.json"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    with open("eval-results.json") as f:
        report = json.load(f)
    pass_rate = report["summary"]["passed"] / report["summary"]["total"]
    print(f"Eval pass rate: {pass_rate:.1%}")
    return pass_rate >= 0.95

if __name__ == "__main__":
    vulns = load_vulnerabilities("pip-audit-report.json")
    if not vulns:
        print("No patches needed.")
        sys.exit(0)
    upgraded = apply_patches(vulns)
    if not run_regression_suite():
        print("Regression suite failed — patch may have introduced a breaking change.")
        sys.exit(1)
    if not run_eval_suite():
        print("Eval suite degraded after patch — verify LLM SDK compatibility.")
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"Patched {len(upgraded)} packages. All tests pass.")
Step 4: Add post-patch GenAI compatibility tests

CRITICAL patch example: pip-audit detects CVE-2024-XXXX in the jinja2 prompt templating library, classified as prompt injection (genAI override: CRITICAL). An alert fires to the on-call engineer. A patch branch is created automatically, jinja2 is upgraded to the patched version, regression and eval suites pass. The on-call engineer reviews the patch branch (5 minutes) and merges. The emergency deployment process deploys to production within 36 hours of CVE detection.

MEDIUM batch example: Monthly dependency scan identifies three MEDIUM CVEs in urllib3, certifi, and requests. These are batched into a single patch PR targeting the next monthly release. The standard regression suite passes; no eval suite run is required. The PR is reviewed in the weekly dependency review meeting and merged with the monthly release.

A legal research firm operating an AI contract analysis agent discovered that a MEDIUM-rated CVE (CVSS 5.8) in their prompt templating library actually enabled indirect prompt injection via document content — an attacker could embed instructions in a contract document that would cause the agent to exfiltrate extracted data to an attacker-controlled endpoint. The CVSS score reflected the severity of the templating vulnerability in isolation, not its impact in an agentic context.

The firm implemented genAI-specific CVSS overrides after this incident. Vulnerabilities in prompt templating libraries are now evaluated against the agentic risk model (not just CVSS), and those that enable prompt injection in agent context are treated as CRITICAL regardless of base score. The same CVE, reclassified as CRITICAL, would have been patched in 48 hours instead of remaining open for 25 days (which was the actual resolution time before the policy change).
Using CVSS alone for genAI patches:

CVSS scores do not account for the agentic context. A prompt injection vulnerability with CVSS 5.0 is more dangerous in an agent than a buffer overflow with CVSS 8.0 in a stateless service. Add genAI-specific classification rules.
Skipping eval suite after LLM SDK patches:

LLM SDK updates frequently change default parameters, response formats, or error handling behaviour. Standard unit tests will not catch these changes; only an eval suite that exercises the agent's actual LLM interactions will.
If dependencies are not version-pinned, pip install --upgrade may upgrade to a version newer than the patch target and introduce unintended changes. Always patch to a specific patched version.

CRITICAL patches applied under emergency processes often have abbreviated testing. A post-patch review within 48 hours of the emergency deployment must verify that the patch did not introduce regressions that were missed under time pressure.
Not testing retrieval quality after vector DB patches:
Vector database patches may change index formats, quantisation algorithms, or distance calculations. Always validate retrieval recall against a reference dataset after a vector DB version change.
Run CVE scans in CI on every PR:
In addition to the daily scheduled scan, run pip-audit on every PR. This catches vulnerabilities introduced by new direct dependencies before they merge.
Maintain a lock file and update it deliberately:
Pin all dependencies to specific versions in requirements.txt or requirements.lock. Patches are applied by deliberate version bumps, not by running pip install without pins.
Review open CVEs and their SLA deadlines in the weekly dependency review meeting. CVEs approaching their SLA deadline are escalated to engineering leadership.
Test patches in staging before production:
Even CRITICAL patches should be deployed to staging and validated before production whenever the SLA allows. Document the staging soak period in the patch process.
Use Dependabot or Renovate to automatically create patch PRs with the CVE details populated in the PR description. This reduces the manual work of creating patch branches.

The daily CVE scan for a typical agentic AI platform (30-60 Python packages) completes in under 2 minutes. The post-patch eval suite is the dominant time cost: a comprehensive eval suite may take 15-30 minutes. For this reason, the eval suite should run only when a patch affects LLM-related packages, triggered by the package name matcher in the policy configuration.

As the dependency graph grows (more packages, more services), the scan time increases proportionally. For systems with hundreds of dependencies, run pip-audit in parallel across requirements files using subprocess.Popen or a parallel CI job matrix.
OSV and NVD database; fast; CI-friendly
More configurable than Dependabot; policy YAML
Daily cron job for dependency scans

The patch process itself must be secure. A compromised dependency scanner or patch script could be used to introduce malicious packages under the guise of security patching. Protect the patch pipeline by: running dependency scans only in isolated CI environments, requiring two-engineer review for any patch that bumps a dependency beyond the minimum patched version, and verifying package checksums against the PyPI signing database.
Enable signature verification in pip (pip install --require-hashes) for production deployments. The requirements.lock file should include SHA256 hashes for every package.
The daily CVE scan job should run with read-only access to the repository and write access only to the scan results artifact. It should not have deployment permissions.
Log all patch PRs with the author identity. A sudden increase in patches from an unexpected author may indicate a compromised automated tooling account.
For CRITICAL emergency patches, verify before deployment that rolling back to the pre-patch version is possible without data loss or session corruption.

Think of the security patch cadence as a triage system for a hospital emergency department. CRITICAL injuries are treated immediately (48 hours) regardless of the queue. HIGH injuries are treated in the urgent care track (7 days). MEDIUM and LOW injuries are scheduled for the next available appointment (30 and 90 days). The genAI-specific overrides are the triage nurse's judgment that overrides the initial severity assessment when the context reveals a higher risk than the standard classification suggests.

The eval suite requirement for LLM SDK patches is the equivalent of a post-surgery compatibility check: the patient (agent) must demonstrate that they can still perform their expected functions after the procedure (patch). A surgical patient who passes post-operative vitals but cannot speak is not recovered; an agent that passes unit tests but fails eval cases has regressed in the same way.
CVSS scores do not account for agentic AI context; add genAI-specific override rules that treat prompt injection vulnerabilities as CRITICAL regardless of base score.
LLM SDK patches always require a full eval suite run to detect silent API behaviour regressions that unit tests will not catch.
Vector database patches require retrieval quality validation (recall@k) to detect index format changes that degrade retrieval accuracy.
CRITICAL patches must be applied within 48 hours; an emergency change process with abbreviated review (but not no review) enables this timeline.
Automate CVE scanning in CI on every PR and on a daily schedule; automate patch PR creation with Dependabot or Renovate.
Pin all dependencies to specific versions so that patches are deliberate version bumps, not accidental updates from unpinned installs.
